title: Why big super is investing your money in AI despite ‘biggest risk’
full_text:
The biggest risk for markets right now is the overwhelming concentration of AI stocks, according to one of the nation’s largest superannuation funds, Aware Super.
But the fear of missing out doesn’t discriminate. The $210bn super fund was one of the 1000 or so institutional investors that nabbed a chunk of SpaceX’s IPO.
“The expectation was it was going to be a really well-supported IPO, centring the index, and we wanted to make sure that we had a position there,” Aware head of investment strategy Michael Winchester said.
“It’s a very small position,” he said, while declining to comment on whether the fund plans to tip money into the looming Anthropic and OpenAI IPOs.
Aware, like others, is grappling with the sky-high valuations commanded by tech giants and the corresponding fears of an AI bubble.
“It’s clear that AI will change the global economy, it will change how we work, but there’s a lot of hopes and dreams in those valuations and that’s probably the single biggest risk in markets today,” Mr Winchester said.
“We’re heading into a year now where the big tech stocks really need to show some results for all the capex they’ve been investing in this AI thematic.”
SpaceX’s share price moves over the past two weeks underscore the wild ride investors are in for. After a short-lived pop, the stock is down more than 20 per cent from its highs, to be trading just above the $US150 ($218) it hit on debut. It issued shares at $US135 for IPO investors.
Other major tech stocks, including AI and chip-related plays, have also been sold off in recent weeks amid ongoing valuation concerns.
Mega floats, market froth, and whether investing in SpaceX risky or smart
Aware’s move to grab even a sliver of SpaceX comes after a challenging year for the fund that saw it deliver lower returns than its peers, in part due to an underweight position in US tech.
As the end of the financial year approaches, Aware is looking at about an 8 per cent return for members, below the median 9 per cent of its peers and well off the roughly 25 per cent global equities delivered over the same 12 months.
Peers Australian Retirement Trust and AustralianSuper should come in right around the middle of the pack; both are tracking just below 9 per cent returns, with a couple of trading days left before the end of financial year.
ART’s investment team, managing $330bn in assets, is also weighing the impact of lofty AI company valuations, as it eyes a looming exposure to SpaceX.
AI protectionism has emerged as a major risk to valuations of these next-gen tech companies, particularly in recent weeks following the US government’s move to shut down Anthropic’s latest AI models over security concerns, ART head of investment strategy Andrew Fisher told The Australian.
“The US (AI companies) serve the world, and that’s why they trade at significant multiples to Chinese AI companies with similar earnings. Anything that restricts the consumer universe risks that premium,” Mr Fisher said.
“Data sovereignty is a big theme now playing through.
“Being pragmatic about it, there’s probably an overinvestment in data centres and data storage versus what might be required in a more efficient market, and a lot of that is coming about because every nation wants sovereignty over their own data.”
While ART currently has no exposure to SpaceX, OpenAI or Anthropic, the fund will hold these stocks as soon as they land in index funds.
For SpaceX, this will likely be within a matter of days. The timeline for Anthropic and OpenAI is less clear, with neither firm yet confirming a date to list on public markets.
In a year when tech giants powered global equities to returns above 25 per cent, Australia’s major funds are hoping this year will mark a turning point for under-pressure private markets.
“The longer public markets outperform private by this level, the sooner we get to a point where either private catches up or public comes back,” Mr Fisher said.
“These things move in cycles and three years is a fairly long period of time where this sort of cycle would be favouring markets so significantly.”
The Australian sharemarket was a further weight on super returns this year: the ASX 200, including dividends, is up just 6 per cent over 12 months.
“It’s underperformed because we don’t have the same kind of exposure to the AI thematic as in other markets but if we see a sell-off in AI, you would think the Australian market would be seen as a more defensive option,” Mr Winchester said.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
